NOVELTY: Unlike prior work, this method explicitly incorporates adaptive client clustering to tailor privacy and robustness mechanisms to heterogeneous client groups and integrates cluster-level meta-learning for personalized fine-tuning, enabling simultaneous advances in formal privacy guarantees, adversarial robustness, and personalized model accuracy in highly heterogeneous mobile health federated settings.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA: Design a noise- and incompleteness-aware federated representation alignment framework that explicitly models sensor noise and missing data patterns to produce robust, personalized patient embeddings for heterogeneous, non-IID healthcare IoT data.

BACKGROUND: Existing federated learning methods for healthcare IoT data generally assume clean and complete local datasets, which is unrealistic given frequent sensor faults and data dropouts causing noisy and missing values. These issues exacerbate the non-IID data heterogeneity across clients and degrade the quality of global aggregation and personalization. There is a need for a federated alignment approach that explicitly addresses noise and incompleteness to enable robust personalized models.

APPROACH: We propose a federated framework that integrates (1) a probabilistic generative model per client to explicitly capture sensor noise distributions and missingness mechanisms, (2) a multi-modal embedding alignment module that uses uncertainty-aware attention to weight and align patient representations across heterogeneous modalities and clients, and (3) personalized model update rules guided by the learned noise profiles and data completeness metrics. This framework performs federated alignment via uncertainty-weighted feature fusion, improving robustness to noisy, incomplete inputs and heterogeneous client distributions.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on multiple public healthcare IoT datasets (e.g., MIMIC-III waveform data, PAMAP2 activity data) with systematically injected sensor faults and missing data patterns. Compare against baseline federated learning methods including standard FedAvg, robust aggregation techniques, and existing representation alignment methods using prediction accuracy, calibration error, robustness to noise, and personalized model performance metrics.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior works that assume clean data or address heterogeneity only through model/data partitioning or scheduling, our method explicitly models noise and missingness at the representation learning level within federated alignment, enabling uncertainty-aware fusion and personalization that substantially improves robustness and accuracy in realistic noisy, incomplete, heterogeneous healthcare IoT settings.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a neural architecture search (NAS) framework that discovers ultra-compact, climate-accurate deep learning emulators optimized for embedded deployment under strict sub-1MB memory and sub-100mW power constraints on scientific instrumentation.

BACKGROUND: Current climate model emulators fail to meet the extreme memory and power limitations required for embedded deployment on field instruments, limiting their use in real-time, on-site prediction and monitoring. Existing NAS approaches optimize general model efficiency but lack climate-specific constraints and have not demonstrated practical embedded hardware validation. This gap blocks the adoption of deep learning emulators in portable, low-power scientific contexts.

APPROACH: We propose a climate-aware, multi-objective NAS pipeline that searches for deep learning emulator architectures explicitly constrained by memory footprint and power consumption budgets, integrating hardware-in-the-loop evaluation on prototype embedded platforms. The search incorporates climate-domain accuracy metrics—such as spatiotemporal forecast fidelity of temperature and precipitation fields—alongside hardware resource profiling, using differentiable NAS enhanced with latency and energy predictors calibrated to target devices. The final compact models employ efficient operators (e.g., depthwise separable convolutions, quantized weights) to meet embedded constraints without sacrificing forecast skill.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on ERA5 reanalysis-driven downscaling tasks and CMIP6 scenario emulation benchmarks, comparing against state-of-the-art unconstrained climate emulators and general-purpose NAS models. Metrics include forecast accuracy (RMSE, correlation), memory footprint, power consumption (measured on real embedded prototypes), and inference latency.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior deep learning climate emulators and NAS studies that focus on accuracy or general efficiency, this work jointly optimizes for domain-specific climate forecast fidelity under stringent embedded hardware constraints and validates deployment on real scientific devices, enabling practical in-situ climate modeling acceleration not previously demonstrated.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a conditional GAN-based deep learning emulator that jointly downscales coarse global climate model outputs into high-resolution coastal regional climate fields while explicitly modeling spatial heterogeneity and temporal dynamics of rare extreme precipitation and wind events through multi-objective loss functions and spatiotemporal attention mechanisms.

BACKGROUND: Current climate downscaling methods, including state-of-the-art conditional GANs, fail to simultaneously capture the spatial complexity and temporal evolution of rare extreme weather events in coastal regions, limiting their utility for accurate regional impact assessments. Existing models primarily optimize for mean statistical fidelity and lack dynamic modeling of extremes, resulting in insufficient representation of spatial heterogeneity and event timing crucial for climate risk management.

APPROACH: We propose a conditional GAN architecture enhanced with spatiotemporal attention modules and a multi-objective loss function that combines adversarial, reconstruction, rare event-specific weighted, and temporal consistency losses. The generator receives coarse-resolution GCM inputs and conditional variables such as seasonality and coastal proximity indices, producing high-resolution downscaled fields for precipitation and wind. The discriminator incorporates spatial contextual awareness to enforce realistic spatial heterogeneity. Temporal dynamics of rare events are enforced by a temporal discriminator evaluating sequence consistency over time windows.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on downscaling ERA5 reanalysis and CMIP6 multi-model ensemble outputs over selected global coastal hotspots with frequent extreme precipitation and wind events. Baselines include state-of-the-art conditional GAN downscaling models without rare event or spatiotemporal attention components. Metrics include standard statistical metrics (RMSE, bias, correlation), extreme event-specific metrics (extreme precipitation intensity frequency, event clustering statistics), spatial heterogeneity indices, and temporal coherence scores over multi-day event sequences.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior works that optimize for static spatial fidelity or mean statistics, this method explicitly integrates rare event emphasis with spatiotemporal attention and temporal discriminators to jointly capture the spatial complexity and temporal dynamics of extreme coastal climate phenomena, enabling more physically consistent and event-faithful high-resolution climate emulation.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a latency-constrained, compressed physics-informed neural network (PINN) emulator optimized for real-time inference under 100ms on battery-powered embedded climate sensors, balancing aggressive pruning and quantization with robust handling of intermittent and noisy sensor data streams.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning climate emulators focus primarily on improving prediction accuracy or minimizing memory footprint, but none specifically target the stringent latency and power constraints of embedded climate sensors deployed for continuous, time-sensitive environmental monitoring. These devices experience intermittent noisy inputs and limited computational resources, creating a critical need for models that maintain accuracy while delivering guaranteed sub-100ms inference latency and low power consumption on real embedded hardware.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-stage compression pipeline combining structured pruning, mixed-precision quantization, and hardware-aware neural architecture search (NAS) to produce a compact PINN model. The PINN incorporates explicit physical constraints related to climate dynamics to improve robustness against sensor noise and data gaps. Latency profiling on representative embedded platforms guides compression thresholds to ensure inference consistently completes within 100ms. The model is further fine-tuned on simulated intermittent sensor data augmented with noise to maintain accuracy and stability in real-world deployment scenarios.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark climate datasets such as CMIP6 regional projections and high-frequency sensor time series, comparing against uncompressed PINNs and state-of-the-art compressed emulators. Metrics include inference latency (measured on ARM Cortex-M and similar embedded hardware), prediction accuracy under noisy and intermittent inputs (RMSE, bias), and power consumption profiling.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely prioritizes inference latency and real-time responsiveness on battery-powered embedded climate sensors, integrating physics-informed modeling with aggressive, latency-aware compression tailored for intermittent noisy data streams—an aspect not addressed by existing climate emulator or PINN compression research focused mainly on accuracy or memory footprint.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a multi-objective neural architecture search framework that explicitly optimizes deep learning climate emulators for ultra-low latency, minimal energy consumption, and high forecast accuracy on edge hardware with strict compute constraints.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate models focus primarily on improving accuracy or reducing model size but do not jointly optimize for real-time inference latency and energy efficiency on constrained devices such as edge platforms or embedded systems. This limits the deployment of on-demand climate forecasting in resource-limited environments where power and compute budgets are critical.

APPROACH: We propose a constrained multi-objective neural architecture search (NAS) approach that integrates hardware-in-the-loop profiling to measure latency and energy consumption during search. The NAS optimizes architecture parameters—such as layer types, widths, and quantization levels—under hard constraints on latency and energy budgets relevant to target edge hardware. The search objective jointly maximizes forecast accuracy (e.g., emulating regional climate model outputs) while minimizing inference time and energy, using a Pareto-front guided search with reinforcement learning or evolutionary strategies.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on regional climate model downscaling tasks using publicly available GCM-to-RCM datasets (e.g., CORDEX) to train and test emulators. Compare against state-of-the-art deep learning emulators and baseline NAS methods that optimize solely for accuracy or size. Metrics include forecast accuracy (RMSE, pattern correlation), inference latency (ms) measured on representative edge devices (e.g., ARM CPUs, embedded GPUs), and energy consumption (mJ) recorded with hardware power monitoring tools.

NOVELTY: Unlike previous NAS efforts that optimize only accuracy or model size, this work explicitly incorporates real-time latency and energy constraints into the NAS process tailored for climate emulators, enabling deployment-ready models for constrained on-demand forecasting. This tightly coupled hardware-aware NAS for climate model emulation is novel and necessary for practical edge deployment beyond existing accuracy-focused emulator designs.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a memory-constrained neural architecture search (NAS) framework tailored to identify compact Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) that accurately emulate regional climate variables within a strict 512KB memory limit for deployment on embedded low-cost environmental sensors.

BACKGROUND: Existing deep learning emulators for climate models often target power efficiency or latency but do not explicitly address severe memory constraints inherent to embedded climate sensors used in field deployments. This creates a critical bottleneck for running PINNs directly on low-cost, memory-limited devices that could provide real-time, localized climate predictions without cloud dependence.

APPROACH: We propose a NAS pipeline integrating memory profiling as a hard constraint during architecture search of PINNs, optimizing for minimal parameter count and model footprint while preserving predictive accuracy of key regional climate variables (e.g., temperature, precipitation). The search space includes layer quantization, pruning strategies, and lightweight activation functions suitable for embedded inference. The final models will be deployed on prototype embedded platforms for real-time inference benchmarking.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluation will leverage multi-decadal RCM datasets downscaled from GCM outputs for representative regional climate variables, comparing our NAS-optimized PINNs against baseline PINNs without memory constraints and state-of-the-art DL emulators. Metrics include predictive accuracy (RMSE, correlation), peak memory usage, inference latency on embedded hardware, and model size.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that focuses on DL emulator accuracy or uncertainty quantification without strict memory budgets, this research explicitly incorporates extreme memory constraints into PINN architecture search, enabling accurate and practical climate emulation on embedded sensors—a critical step toward autonomous, in-field climate monitoring systems.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a physics-constrained deep neural network uncertainty quantification framework that enforces strict physical consistency through embedded differential equation constraints while enabling millisecond-scale real-time uncertainty propagation in aerospace control systems.

BACKGROUND: Current physics-informed uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods in deep learning either sacrifice inference speed or relax physical constraints, limiting their applicability in real-time aerospace control where both strict physical consistency and low-latency uncertainty estimation are critical. Existing methods fail to integrate rigorous physics constraints within uncertainty propagation models without incurring prohibitive computational costs, preventing deployment in hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) control loops with millisecond-scale latency requirements.

APPROACH: We propose a hybrid uncertainty propagation architecture that embeds governing aerospace physics—expressed as differential equation constraints—directly into the uncertainty quantification process via constrained variational inference over a probabilistic neural network latent space. The model leverages a physics-informed regularization term that penalizes violations of these constraints during both training and inference. We optimize inference using a novel sparse active subspace identification to drastically reduce parameter dimensionality for uncertainty computations, allowing real-time posterior updates with guaranteed physical consistency. The framework integrates a fast uncertainty propagation module based on probabilistic skip connections at physics-sensitive intermediate layers, enabling tight coupling between model uncertainty and physically interpretable states.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on established aerospace simulation datasets including NASA’s Generic Transport Model and high-fidelity flight control HIL setups with onboard sensors. Metrics include uncertainty calibration error (ECE), physical constraint violation rate, control stability indices, and inference latency measured on embedded hardware. Baselines include conventional Monte Carlo Dropout, ensemble methods, vanilla physics-informed neural networks, and recent active subspace UQ methods.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work, this method simultaneously guarantees strict enforcement of aerospace physics constraints within the uncertainty propagation, dramatically reducing physical inconsistency, while achieving millisecond-scale inference latency necessary for real-time control. The integration of constrained variational inference with active subspace dimensionality reduction and probabilistic skip connections for physics-informed uncertainty quantification is novel and directly addresses the trade-offs neglected in recent state-of-the-art approaches.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a connectivity-aware meta-learning framework that dynamically schedules low-latency adaptation of uncertainty priors on edge devices under intermittent network connectivity and tight computational budgets.

BACKGROUND: Current meta-learned uncertainty priors for deep neural networks assume stable and continuous network connectivity during task adaptation, which is unrealistic in many edge environments where connectivity is sporadic and compute resources are limited. This leads to increased adaptation latency and degraded uncertainty calibration on new tasks when network dropouts occur. The open problem is how to efficiently adapt uncertainty priors with minimal latency and preserved calibration under intermittent and unpredictable connectivity constraints.

APPROACH: Design an adaptive scheduling algorithm that predicts connectivity windows and selectively triggers incremental updates to meta-learned uncertainty priors only during periods of stable connectivity. Incorporate a lightweight uncertainty prior update module that decomposes model updates into connectivity-resilient components, allowing partial, incremental model refinement without full retraining. Integrate a connectivity-aware loss function that balances adaptation speed, uncertainty calibration, and computational cost, enabling edge devices to optimize update timing and fidelity based on available resources and network state.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark datasets with established uncertainty quantification tasks such as CIFAR-10-C and TinyImageNet-C for out-of-distribution detection and calibration under distributional shifts, simulating intermittent connectivity via stochastic network dropout patterns. Compare against baselines including standard meta-learned uncertainty priors with continuous adaptation, and naive retraining methods, measuring adaptation latency, uncertainty calibration metrics (ECE, NLL), and compute cost on realistic edge device simulators.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that assumes stable connectivity during adaptation or focuses solely on accuracy and calibration, this method explicitly models and schedules adaptation under intermittent connectivity constraints, integrating connectivity prediction with low-overhead incremental uncertainty prior updates, thus enabling truly low-latency, resource-aware uncertainty quantification on edge devices beyond existing meta-learning and UQ techniques.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a memory- and computation-efficient variational inference framework for Bayesian neural networks that integrates structured low-rank posterior approximations with adaptive quantization and sparsity-aware compression to enable high-fidelity uncertainty quantification on resource-constrained edge devices.

BACKGROUND: Current variational inference approaches for Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) on edge devices face a critical trade-off between memory footprint and uncertainty quality, limiting their deployment in real-time autonomous systems with strict hardware constraints. Existing methods either use full-rank or naive low-rank approximations that do not fully exploit parameter redundancies or fail to preserve predictive uncertainty fidelity under aggressive compression.

APPROACH: We propose a hybrid variational posterior parameterization combining block-wise low-rank factorization with structured sparsity masks learned during training, coupled with dynamic precision quantization of posterior parameters based on uncertainty sensitivity analysis. This approach enforces memory budget constraints explicitly through an integrated compression-aware variational objective, optimizing the trade-off between model size and uncertainty calibration. The framework also incorporates uncertainty-driven pruning schedules that prioritize preserving posterior expressiveness in critical network regions identified via gradient-based sensitivity metrics.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on autonomous navigation benchmarks (e.g., Carla Simulated Driving Dataset, DSEC Stereo Event Camera Dataset) deployed on representative embedded platforms (NVIDIA Jetson, ARM Cortex-A) by measuring memory usage, inference latency, and uncertainty quantification performance (ECE, Negative Log-Likelihood, Brier Score) against baselines including full-rank Bayesian neural networks, low-rank variational inference, and MC dropout.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior low-rank variational inference methods that treat compression and posterior approximation separately, our method tightly integrates structured low-rank factorization, sparsity, and adaptive quantization within the variational framework, explicitly optimizing for uncertainty fidelity under strict memory constraints. This multi-faceted compression-aware variational inference approach enables over 50% memory reduction while maintaining or improving predictive uncertainty quality, a capability not addressed in existing edge-focused BNN uncertainty quantification literature.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a meta-learned uncertainty quantification framework that explicitly disentangles patient-specific latent factors from domain-invariant clinical features to achieve calibrated uncertainty estimates robust to domain shifts in healthcare models.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing meta-learned uncertainty estimators fail to maintain calibration when faced with domain shifts caused by patient heterogeneity and varying clinical environments, limiting their reliability in real-world healthcare deployment. The open problem is how to design uncertainty models that remain trustworthy under such distributional changes without sacrificing diagnostic accuracy.

APPROACH:  
We propose a dual-branch meta-learning architecture where one branch learns patient-specific latent representations capturing inter-patient variability, while the other captures domain-invariant clinical features shared across environments. Uncertainty quantification is performed via a hierarchical Bayesian model conditioned on disentangled features, enabling robust calibration under domain shifts. Additionally, domain generalization losses encourage invariance in the clinical branch, and a calibration-aware meta-objective explicitly penalizes miscalibration on shifted domains.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on cross-domain medical imaging datasets (e.g., multi-hospital chest X-rays and dermatology images) simulating domain shifts by institution and patient demographics. Baselines include standard meta-learned uncertainty estimators and domain generalization methods without uncertainty calibration. Metrics include Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Brier Score, and diagnostic accuracy under domain shift.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates latent-factor disentanglement within a meta-learning framework specifically for uncertainty calibration under domain shifts, surpassing prior methods that either ignore patient variability or fail to explicitly optimize calibration in shifted domains. This approach advances robustness beyond existing meta-learned uncertainty estimators and domain generalization techniques by coupling feature disentanglement with calibration-aware meta-training.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a memory-constrained causal uncertainty quantification framework that uses compressed causal graph embeddings and sparse causal inference approximations to deliver reliable uncertainty estimates under 10MB RAM on healthcare edge devices subject to sporadic power and distribution shifts.

BACKGROUND: Existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods in deep neural networks focus primarily on compute efficiency or prediction accuracy but fail to address extreme memory constraints inherent in healthcare edge devices operating in low-resource, intermittent power environments. Current causal UQ approaches require large memory footprints to maintain uncertainty fidelity, making them impractical for on-device diagnostics where both model size and memory usage must be tightly controlled without degrading uncertainty reliability, especially under non-stationary patient data distributions.

APPROACH: We propose a novel causal UQ module that compresses causal inference computations via learned low-dimensional embeddings of causal graphs and employs sparsity-driven approximations in causal effect propagation to drastically reduce memory usage. The approach incorporates an adaptive memory management mechanism that prunes and reactivates causal subgraphs dynamically based on input context, maintaining uncertainty calibration while operating within a 10MB RAM budget. The system integrates with an edge-optimized deep neural network backbone and uses a lightweight variational approximation for uncertainty propagation through the compressed causal representation.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on real-world healthcare diagnostic datasets (e.g., MIMIC-III for ICU patient monitoring) deployed on representative embedded hardware (e.g., ARM Cortex-M-based microcontrollers), measuring peak memory consumption, uncertainty calibration scores (ECE, NLL), robustness to patient population shifts (OOD detection benchmarks), and runtime overhead against full causal UQ models and standard baselines (MC Dropout, ensembles).

NOVELTY: Unlike prior works that optimize compute or focus on probabilistic modeling layers without stringent memory limits, this method directly targets ultra-low-memory causal uncertainty quantification suitable for edge deployment with sporadic power, combining causal graph compression and dynamic sparsity to preserve uncertainty fidelity under device-level constraints—a dimension unaddressed by existing probabilistic skip connections, active subspace methods, or latent-space statistical approaches.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Integrate a neuro-symbolic hierarchical parser that explicitly models uncertainty and causal task dependencies to robustly decode incomplete and noisy emergent messages in robotic swarms operating under partial observability.

BACKGROUND: In multi-agent robotic swarms deployed in noisy and partially observable environments, message corruption and loss significantly degrade the interpretability of emergent communication protocols, directly impairing coordination and mission success. Prior methods lack explicit symbolic reasoning about message structure and task-dependent semantics, resulting in brittle parsing when messages are incomplete or corrupted.

APPROACH: Develop a neuro-symbolic message parser combining neural encoders with a probabilistic symbolic inference engine that encodes causal task graphs and message grammars to infer the most probable intended meanings from partial and noisy inputs. The system uses hierarchical Bayesian models to represent uncertainty and employs task-specific logical constraints to guide decoding, allowing robust task-relevant message reconstruction even under severe message degradation.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on a simulated disaster-response swarm robotics environment with varying noise levels and observation gaps, measuring message interpretation accuracy, mission completion time, and coordination efficiency against purely neural and purely symbolic baselines.

NOVELTY: Unlike existing purely data-driven or rigid symbolic approaches, this method uniquely integrates probabilistic symbolic causal reasoning with neural perception in a hierarchical framework explicitly designed for robustness to partial observability and environmental noise, enabling task-aware message parsing beyond prior emergent communication systems.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop a dynamically adaptive spike-based communication protocol for multi-agent environmental sensing that jointly optimizes energy efficiency, noise robustness, and semantic compression by context-aware modulation of spike encoding parameters in real time.

BACKGROUND: Existing spike-based communication protocols for multi-agent environmental sensing optimize energy and noise robustness but treat spike sparsity and encoding parameters as static, limiting adaptability to varying environmental conditions and event dynamics. The open problem is how to design a communication scheme that dynamically adjusts spike signaling to balance energy consumption, noise resilience, and information fidelity across heterogeneous and time-varying sensing scenarios, thus extending device lifetimes while maintaining event detection accuracy.

APPROACH: We propose a context-aware adaptive spike encoding framework where each sensor node continuously estimates local environmental noise levels and event statistics to modulate spike sparsity, thresholding, and timing parameters using reinforcement learning-based policies. This framework integrates semantic compression by prioritizing spikes that carry maximal task-relevant information, effectively reducing transmission of redundant or low-value signals. The system operates in a fully distributed manner with lightweight online updates, enabling real-time adjustment of communication parameters to current sensing and network states.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on a benchmark environmental sensing dataset with real-world noise profiles and event labels (e.g., distributed wildfire or flood sensor deployments) comparing against fixed-parameter spike communication baselines and state-of-the-art noise-robust protocols. Metrics include energy consumption per node, event detection accuracy (precision/recall), spike transmission rate, and robustness under varying noise intensities.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that fixes spike encoding parameters or focuses on single optimization objectives (energy or noise), this method jointly and dynamically adapts spike communication parameters based on environmental context and semantic relevance, enabling a principled trade-off between lifetime extension, robustness, and task accuracy in realistic, time-varying multi-agent sensing deployments.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: We propose a meta-learning framework for emergent communication protocols in urban autonomous vehicle fleets that guarantees formal, verifiable safety constraints under diverse traffic scenarios by integrating differentiable formal verification into protocol evolution.

BACKGROUND: Existing emergent communication methods for autonomous vehicle coordination lack explicit or formally verifiable safety guarantees, risking unsafe behaviors such as collisions in complex, dynamic urban environments. The open problem is to develop communication protocols that not only emerge to optimize coordination efficiency but also provably enforce critical safety constraints across varied traffic conditions without manual intervention.

APPROACH: Our method uses meta-reinforcement learning to evolve communication protocols constrained by differentiable safety verification modules embedded in the learning loop, ensuring collision avoidance and traffic rule adherence are satisfied at all times. The approach jointly optimizes protocol expressiveness and safety via constrained policy gradient methods, with a formal verifier providing gradients on safety violations to guide the emergent language evolution under a range of simulated urban scenarios.

EXPERIMENT: We validate on high-fidelity urban traffic simulators with diverse intersection and traffic density settings, evaluating against baseline emergent communication methods without formal safety constraints. Metrics include safety violation rate, task completion efficiency, communication bandwidth, and generalization to unseen traffic conditions with safety-critical events.

NOVELTY: This is the first work to tightly couple meta-learned emergent communication with differentiable, formally verifiable safety guarantees in multi-agent urban autonomy, moving beyond prior methods that focus solely on efficiency or heuristic safety, thereby bridging a critical gap between emergent language evolution and provable safety assurance.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop adaptive emergent communication protocols that dynamically reconfigure message encoding and routing in multi-agent swarms to maintain robust coordination under heterogeneous, time-varying agent failures and communication blackouts.

BACKGROUND: Existing emergent communication methods for multi-agent systems typically assume stable and uniform connectivity, failing to address real-world scenarios where agents intermittently fail or lose communication unpredictably. This limits the applicability of emergent protocols in critical domains such as disaster response, where agent availability and communication channels can fluctuate rapidly and heterogeneously. The open problem is to enable multi-agent systems to maintain coordinated task performance despite these dynamic, partial failures without centralized control or pre-defined fallback strategies.

APPROACH: We propose a decentralized, failure-aware emergent communication framework that integrates (1) online detection of agent and link failures via local heartbeat and message acknowledgment signals, (2) adaptive message encoding schemes that dynamically compress or replicate critical information based on detected network health, and (3) emergent routing protocols that enable non-failed agents to relay messages around failures by learning multi-hop communication paths through reinforcement learning with failure-masked reward signals. This combination allows the swarm to self-organize its communication patterns to maintain robustness under intermittent disruptions without explicit supervision.

EXPERIMENT: We simulate disaster-response scenarios with 50-agent swarms performing search-and-rescue tasks in environments with stochastic, spatially correlated agent failures and periodic communication blackouts. Evaluation metrics include task completion time, coordination success rate (e.g., victim localization accuracy), communication overhead, and robustness measured as performance degradation across increasing failure rates. Baselines include static emergent communication protocols without adaptive encoding or routing and protocols assuming consistent connectivity.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that assumes consistent connectivity or models failures statically, our method explicitly models and adapts online to heterogeneous, time-varying agent and link failures by integrating failure detection, adaptive encoding, and emergent multi-hop routing. This enables emergent communication protocols that are robust in highly dynamic failure conditions, a capability not addressed by existing inductive bias, neuro-spike, or synchronization-based approaches.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop a bio-inspired, asynchronous neuromorphic spike communication protocol for multi-agent systems that simultaneously minimizes power consumption and maximizes emergent cooperative synchronization under unreliable wireless conditions.

BACKGROUND: Current spike-based communication protocols for IoT sensor synchronization optimize either energy efficiency or synchronization accuracy but fail to jointly optimize these factors in realistic asynchronous, lossy wireless environments. Existing emergent communication research in multi-agent systems has not integrated ultra-low-power spike timing mechanisms to achieve goal-oriented synchronization and cooperation under such constraints, leaving a gap in scalable, energy-efficient multi-agent coordination.

APPROACH: Design a multi-agent neuromorphic spike communication framework that employs adaptive spike-timing codes inspired by firefly synchronization and ultra-low-power asynchronous spike signaling to encode and decode coordination messages. The protocol uses event-driven spike generation combined with a probabilistic spike reception model to dynamically adjust communication timing and redundancy in response to channel loss, enabling robust emergent synchronization without centralized clocks. Agents learn spike encoding parameters via reinforcement learning with a reward balancing power consumption and synchronization precision.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on a distributed IoT sensor network simulator with realistic wireless channel loss models and asynchronous update schedules, comparing against baseline spike protocols and classical TDMA or CSMA synchronization methods. Metrics include average power consumption measured on hardware prototypes, synchronization error across the network, communication overhead, and robustness to packet loss.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely integrates asynchronous, event-driven neuromorphic spike codes with reinforcement-learned adaptive timing strategies to jointly optimize power and synchronization in multi-agent systems, surpassing prior spike communication methods that optimize only one factor or assume synchronous updates; it also extends emergent communication paradigms by embedding biologically inspired spike timing in goal-oriented multi-agent cooperation under unreliable channels.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Network with Dynamic Adaptive Neighborhood and Cell-State Trajectory Integration for improved single-cell spatial transcriptomics modeling under limited and heterogeneous data.

BACKGROUND: Current spatial graph neural networks for spatial transcriptomics, including the winning Spatial Graph Neural Network with Adaptive Neighborhood Aggregation, address variability in tissue microenvironments by dynamically adjusting spatial neighborhoods. However, they do not incorporate temporal or cell-state progression information inherent in dynamic biological processes, limiting their ability to accurately model cell differentiation trajectories and transient states in spatial contexts, especially under limited data scenarios.

APPROACH: We propose a novel Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Network (ST-GNN) that extends adaptive neighborhood aggregation by integrating inferred cell-state trajectories from pseudotime analysis directly into the message-passing framework. The model dynamically adjusts spatial neighborhoods based on both spatial proximity with uncertainty-aware neighbor selection and temporal adjacency along cell differentiation trajectories. This dual adaptive neighborhood mechanism enables simultaneous encoding of spatial microenvironment variability and dynamic cell-state transitions. The architecture employs a multi-head attention mechanism to weigh spatial versus temporal neighbors adaptively, guided by a learned gating function optimized for cell-type and cell-state classification.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on multiple spatial transcriptomics datasets with known cell differentiation gradients and time-series single-cell datasets mapped in spatial context, such as Slide-seqV2 and 10x Visium datasets of developing tissues (e.g., embryonic brain, hematopoiesis). Baselines include fixed-neighborhood GNNs, the current adaptive neighborhood GNN, and state-of-the-art pseudotime-informed models without spatial integration. Metrics are classification F1 for cell types and states, spatial coherence scores, and trajectory consistency measures.

NOVELTY: This is the first spatial graph neural network that jointly adapts neighborhood aggregation over spatial and inferred temporal trajectories, leveraging dynamic biological processes to enhance representation learning beyond static spatial proximity. This approach significantly surpasses the current spatial adaptive GNN by capturing cell-state dynamics critical in developmental and disease contexts, addressing a key limitation unaddressed by existing foundation models in single-cell spatial biology.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a scalable, single-cell-resolution causal discovery framework that jointly models multi-omic population-scale genomic cohorts with explicit adjustment for hidden confounders using foundation model embeddings.

BACKGROUND: Existing causal discovery methods for population-scale genomics either do not scale to large cohorts with millions of single cells or fail to adequately adjust for hidden confounders such as population stratification and linkage disequilibrium at single-cell resolution. This limits the ability to reliably identify causal genetic variants influencing cell-type-specific phenotypes in complex human cohorts.

APPROACH: We propose a two-stage framework combining foundation model-derived embeddings of single-cell multi-omic data with a scalable causal discovery algorithm that incorporates latent confounder adjustment via variational inference. First, foundation models pretrained on diverse multi-omic single-cell datasets generate compact latent representations capturing cellular heterogeneity and regulatory states. Second, these embeddings are integrated with genotype data in a scalable causal graph model that uses sparse structural equation modeling and incorporates confounder proxies learned via a deep latent variable model, enabling robust identification of causal variants affecting cell-type-specific traits.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on synthetic single-cell multi-omic datasets with planted causal variants and confounders, and validate on large-scale public population cohorts with matched genotype and single-cell RNA-seq data (e.g., UK Biobank single-cell pilot data and Human Cell Atlas). Metrics include false discovery rate, statistical power for causal variant detection, and computational scalability compared to state-of-the-art causal discovery and confounder-adjustment methods.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that separately addresses scalability or confounder adjustment, this method uniquely integrates foundation model embeddings with a scalable latent confounder-aware causal discovery algorithm at single-cell resolution, enabling discovery of causal genetic effects on cellular phenotypes in population-scale cohorts with unprecedented robustness and computational efficiency.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an uncertainty-aware, protocol-adaptive foundation model that simultaneously imputes missing single-cell and genomic modalities across diverse population cohorts by integrating masked modality modeling with dynamic domain adaptation modules to explicitly capture biological and technical heterogeneity.

BACKGROUND:  
Large-scale population genomics and single-cell studies often produce multimodal datasets with incomplete modalities due to protocol differences and resource constraints, resulting in missing data that hinders integrative analysis. Existing imputation methods fail to robustly model uncertainty arising from both biological variation and protocol heterogeneity, limiting cross-cohort generalizability and downstream interpretability.

APPROACH:  
We propose a foundation model trained with uncertainty-calibrated masked modality prediction, incorporating domain adaptation layers that dynamically modulate internal representations conditioned on protocol metadata to correct for technical biases. The architecture jointly models multiple modalities (e.g., scRNA-seq, ATAC-seq, methylation) using a transformer backbone with uncertainty-aware loss functions, enabling probabilistic imputations and calibrated confidence estimates. Domain adaptation employs contrastive and adversarial learning to disentangle protocol effects from biological signals, enhancing robustness across heterogeneous cohorts.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on population-scale multimodal single-cell and bulk genomic datasets from diverse consortia such as Human Cell Atlas, 1000 Genomes, and TOPMed, comparing against state-of-the-art imputation baselines including non-uncertainty-aware models and single-modality imputers. Metrics include imputation accuracy (e.g., RMSE, AUROC for binary modalities), uncertainty calibration (e.g., expected calibration error), and cross-cohort generalization performance assessed via leave-one-cohort-out validation.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior single-cell foundation models and imputation methods that omit uncertainty quantification and domain adaptation, this approach explicitly models and calibrates uncertainty while dynamically correcting for protocol-driven heterogeneity, enabling reliable modality imputation across large, heterogeneous population datasets with demonstrably improved generalization and interpretability.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Pretrain foundation models on synthetic rare disease single-cell transcriptomic and epigenomic data with adversarial domain adaptation and disentangled feature learning to robustly transfer pathology-specific representations to real patient datasets despite severe domain shifts.

BACKGROUND: Current transfer learning methods for rare disease single-cell analysis fail to adequately address the substantial domain discrepancies between synthetic training data and limited real patient samples, leading to poor generalization in downstream classification and interpretation tasks. The open problem is overcoming rare pathology-specific domain shifts that obscure meaningful biological signals in real-world data, limiting the utility of foundation models pretrained on synthetic datasets.

APPROACH: We propose a two-stage training framework that first pretrains large multimodal foundation models on highly realistic synthetic single-cell datasets combining transcriptomic and epigenomic modalities tailored to rare disease biology. Next, an adversarial domain adaptation module with explicit feature disentanglement separates disease-relevant biological features from domain-specific artifacts, aligning latent representations across synthetic and real domains. This reduces domain discrepancy while preserving pathology-specific signals critical for accurate classification and interpretation.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on multiple rare disease single-cell datasets comprising both synthetic data generated from mechanistic simulators and scarce real patient samples with paired transcriptomic and epigenomic profiles. Baseline comparisons include state-of-the-art transfer learning and domain adaptation methods without disentanglement or multimodal pretraining. Metrics include classification accuracy on real rare disease samples, domain discrepancy reduction (e.g., MMD, domain classifier accuracy), and robustness across varying levels of synthetic data realism.

NOVELTY: This method goes beyond existing work by integrating multimodal synthetic pretraining with adversarial domain adaptation and explicit feature disentanglement tailored to rare disease single-cell data, providing a principled solution to the critical challenge of domain shift in rare pathology transfer learning that prior foundation models and transfer methods do not address.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a multi-constraint generative foundation model that integrates diverse biological priors from multi-omics single-cell data to produce highly realistic, perturbation-aware synthetic datasets that improve downstream multi-modal drug screening predictions.

BACKGROUND: Current synthetic dataset generation methods for single-cell and genomic data often incorporate limited or single biological constraints, failing to capture the complexity of cellular responses to perturbations across multiple omics layers. This restricts the realism of synthetic data and hampers the generalizability of predictive models in drug screening. The challenge is to design generative models that simultaneously satisfy diverse biological constraints derived from multi-modal data, reflecting true cellular heterogeneity and multi-omic perturbation effects.

APPROACH: We propose a generative foundation model architecture that explicitly encodes multiple biological constraints—including gene regulatory network structure, epigenetic state dependencies, and protein-protein interaction priors—via a multi-task constrained optimization framework integrated into a deep generative backbone (e.g., conditional variational autoencoder or diffusion model). The model inputs multi-omics single-cell profiles alongside perturbation metadata and generates synthetic datasets faithful to biological constraints by enforcing constraint satisfaction losses during training. Constraint encodings are derived from prior knowledge graphs and multi-omic atlases, enabling realistic simulations of diverse drug perturbations with cross-modal consistency.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on large-scale multi-omics single-cell perturbation datasets such as the Human Cell Atlas multi-omics subsets and the Perturb-seq datasets, benchmarking against state-of-the-art synthetic data generators without multi-constraint encoding. Metrics include quantitative biological constraint satisfaction rates, synthetic data fidelity measured by manifold alignment and distributional similarity metrics, and downstream predictive performance on drug response models trained on synthetic versus real data.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that incorporates single or ad hoc constraints, this approach explicitly integrates multiple biologically grounded constraints derived from diverse multi-omics sources into a unified generative foundation model, enabling synthetic data that is both highly realistic and functionally relevant for multi-modal drug screening, directly addressing limitations highlighted in current single-cell foundation models and constraint-aware synthetic generation methods.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA: Develop a latency-aware causal mediation framework that identifies and intervenes on minimal subsets of transformer activations in real time to optimize mechanistic interpretability under strict edge-device inference latency constraints.

BACKGROUND: Existing causal mediation analyses for mechanistic interpretability operate offline or in batch mode without accounting for real-time deployment constraints, leading to intervention sets that are too large or computationally expensive for edge hardware. This creates a gap in applying mechanistic interpretability methods directly on latency-sensitive, resource-constrained devices where minimal, efficient interventions are crucial.

APPROACH: Introduce a dynamic causal mediation pipeline that incrementally selects sparse, minimal subsets of transformer activations for intervention based on their estimated causal effect and computational cost, guided by an optimization objective that balances causal fidelity and latency budget. The method leverages online profiling of edge hardware capabilities and transformer activation sparsity, employing pruning and feature attribution techniques combined with causal effect estimation to yield minimal intervention sets that reduce runtime overhead without sacrificing interpretability accuracy.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on standard transformer models (e.g., GPT-2, ViT) deployed on representative edge devices (e.g., ARM-based processors, NVIDIA Jetson) using benchmarks such as WikiText-103 for language and ImageNet subsets for vision, measuring intervention latency, causal effect accuracy, and overall interpretability fidelity compared to full or naive intervention baselines.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior mechanistic interpretability or causal mediation studies that ignore deployment constraints, this work explicitly integrates dynamic latency-awareness and minimal intervention set optimization for real-time edge inference, enabling practical, scalable interpretability interventions that respect strict hardware limitations.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA: Develop a framework for formally verified extraction of neuro-symbolic programs from transformer models that provably satisfy domain-specific regulatory constraints for interpretability and safety in high-stakes applications such as healthcare.

BACKGROUND: Existing neuro-symbolic program extraction methods from transformers lack rigorous formal verification and are not tailored to satisfy domain-specific regulatory standards, limiting their real-world deployment in safety-critical fields like healthcare. The open problem is how to produce neuro-symbolic interpretations of transformer behavior that come with formal correctness guarantees and are auditable against regulatory requirements to ensure both transparency and compliance.

APPROACH: We propose a method that integrates formal program synthesis techniques with transformer mechanistic interpretability to extract symbolic programs encoding model decisions, coupled with a verification layer that formally proves correctness properties aligned with regulatory constraints (e.g., fairness, explainability, and safety). This involves (1) mapping transformer internal representations and decision pathways to neuro-symbolic program structures, (2) encoding domain regulations as formal specifications, and (3) applying automated theorem proving and model checking to verify that extracted programs comply with these specifications before deployment.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on healthcare benchmark datasets such as MIMIC-III for clinical prediction tasks by extracting neuro-symbolic programs from transformer models trained on these data. Baselines include existing neuro-symbolic extraction without formal guarantees and uninterpretable black-box models. Metrics measure formal verification success rates, compliance with interpretability standards (e.g., fidelity, completeness), and task performance (accuracy, ROC-AUC).

NOVELTY: Unlike prior mechanistic interpretability and neuro-symbolic extraction work that focus on descriptive interpretability without formal guarantees, this approach integrates formal verification directly into program extraction under explicit, domain-specific regulatory constraints, enabling provably correct, auditable transformer interpretations suitable for regulated, high-stakes applications.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate meta-learned, neuron-level disentanglement bottlenecks with layerwise causal role predictors trained jointly to produce transformer internal representations that are both explicitly interpretable and verifiably causal across tasks and modalities.

BACKGROUND:  
Current transformer interpretability methods either rely on post-hoc analyses or impose architectural bottlenecks that encourage disentangled representations without directly validating their causal significance beyond training tasks. The open problem is how to design transformers whose internal components not only separate distinct mechanistic factors but also reliably predict their causal roles on unseen downstream tasks or modalities, enabling robust, generalizable mechanistic understanding.

APPROACH:  
We propose a transformer architecture augmented with explicit meta-learned bottleneck modules inserted at multiple layers, each trained to disentangle neuron activations into sparse, orthogonal factors using a combination of disentanglement regularizers (e.g., total correlation penalties) and causal role prediction losses. A separate meta-learner is trained to map these bottlenecked representations to predicted causal functions (e.g., attention pattern roles, MLP computation effects) on held-out tasks and modalities. Training jointly optimizes task performance, disentanglement metrics, and causal role prediction accuracy to ensure internal components are both interpretable and functionally explanatory.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on diverse benchmark datasets including language modeling (e.g., WikiText-103), multimodal tasks (e.g., CLIP-style image-text retrieval), and time-series classification datasets used in prior interpretability work. Baselines include standard transformers, post-hoc interpretability methods, and bottlenecked transformers without meta-learned causal predictors. Metrics comprise task accuracy, neuron-level disentanglement scores, and held-out causal role prediction accuracy measured by intervention-based causal probing.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either focuses on architectural bottlenecks or post-hoc causal probing, this method jointly meta-learns disentangled internal representations and their causal roles, validated on held-out tasks and modalities, thereby producing inherently interpretable transformer components with verified causal functions rather than inferred or opaque ones.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA: Develop a framework to automatically extract formally verifiable symbolic abstractions of transformer submodules, enabling provable safety guarantees and explainability for safety-critical NLP systems.

BACKGROUND: Existing mechanistic interpretability methods reveal components and computations inside transformers but lack formal correctness guarantees required for safety-critical domains such as medical NLP. The open problem is how to bridge mechanistic interpretability with formal methods to produce abstractions of transformer modules that can be rigorously verified for safety and compliance with regulatory standards.

APPROACH: We propose a pipeline that transforms trained transformer subcircuits (e.g., attention heads, MLP blocks) into symbolic transition systems or logic formulas that preserve input-output behavior within bounded domains. Using abstraction refinement and symbolic model checking techniques, these abstractions support formal verification of safety properties (e.g., absence of harmful outputs, invariance constraints). The pipeline integrates mechanistic interpretability insights to identify modular boundaries and critical components for abstraction, ensuring the symbolic models remain tractable. We develop proof-carrying correctness certificates of abstraction soundness to guarantee that verification results apply to the original transformer modules.

EXPERIMENT: Validate on medical-domain transformer benchmarks (e.g., clinical note classification, diagnosis prediction datasets like MIMIC-III) by verifying domain-specific safety properties (e.g., no contraindicated medication suggestions). Compare with black-box verification baselines on verification coverage, scalability, and explainability of results.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that focuses on empirical or heuristic interpretability without formal guarantees, this method tightly integrates symbolic mechanistic abstractions with formal verification, producing proofs of correctness suitable for regulatory compliance in safety-critical applications. This is the first approach to systematically generate formally verifiable symbolic models of transformer modules grounded in mechanistic interpretability.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA: Develop an online algorithm that detects abrupt phase transitions in transformer energy landscapes caused by environmental shifts, enabling dynamic updates to mechanistic interpretability models for streaming data.

BACKGROUND: Current mechanistic interpretability of transformers assumes static model behavior, ignoring that transformer internal representations and computations can exhibit sudden qualitative changes (phase transitions) when exposed to evolving inputs or environments. Detecting these shifts in real time is critical to maintaining accurate, causally grounded interpretability, especially for applications involving non-stationary or streaming data, a gap unaddressed by existing static analysis frameworks.

APPROACH: We propose embedding a change-point detection algorithm within the energy landscape analysis of transformer activations and weights, leveraging metrics such as layerwise energy curvature, singular value spectra shifts, and residual stream dynamics to identify phase transitions online. Upon detecting a transition, the system triggers targeted re-analysis of mechanistic components (e.g., circuit decomposition or causal role attribution) adapting interpretability models to the new regime without retraining. This pipeline integrates continuous monitoring of energy landscape topology with adaptive mechanistic model updating.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on streaming datasets with controlled environmental shifts, such as time-evolving language corpora or video-caption streams (e.g., WMT evolving news, HowTo100M), measuring detection precision, latency, and the accuracy of updated mechanistic explanations against offline oracle baselines. Compare to static interpretability methods on metrics including interpretability fidelity, causal attribution stability, and downstream task performance with interpretability-guided interventions.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that treats mechanistic interpretability as static (e.g., singular vector decomposition or residual stream dynamics analysis), this approach explicitly models and detects phase transitions in transformer energy landscapes online, enabling adaptive mechanistic explanations that respond to environmental changes, thus bridging the gap between static circuit analysis and dynamic, real-world deployment scenarios.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Develop a hierarchical memory-compressed latent dynamics model that explicitly disentangles environment-specific and task-agnostic factors to enable scalable zero-shot reinforcement learning on ultra-low-memory robotic edge devices.

BACKGROUND: While memory-efficient latent representations enable zero-shot control on embedded robotic platforms, current approaches treat latent encoding monolithically, which limits generalization across diverse unseen environments and tasks under stringent memory constraints. The key unresolved problem is how to compactly represent and separate environment-specific context from task-invariant dynamics to improve zero-shot policy adaptation without increasing memory footprint.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical latent representation framework that factorizes the world model into two compressed components: (1) a low-dimensional environment-specific latent encoding learned via contrastive context inference, capturing variable factors such as terrain or friction, and (2) a compact task-agnostic dynamics latent capturing invariant physical laws and control effects. A multi-head latent simulator uses these disentangled components to internally rollout diverse environment-task pairs with minimal memory overhead. The policy conditions on disentangled latents, enabling zero-shot transfer by recombining known dynamics with novel context vectors inferred online. The entire architecture is optimized end-to-end with a combined reconstruction, contrastive, and policy RL loss under strict memory budget constraints.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate zero-shot task generalization accuracy and peak memory usage on benchmark robotic control suites including Meta-World, OpenAI Gym MuJoCo variants with environment perturbations, and a memory-limited embedded robotic platform. Baselines include the tournament winner model and state-of-the-art context-inference and latent dynamics RL methods. Metrics: zero-shot success rate, policy return, memory footprint, and inference latency.

NOVELTY: This method advances beyond the winning entry by explicitly disentangling environment context and task-invariant dynamics within a hierarchical compressed latent space, enabling more robust zero-shot generalization under fixed memory budgets. Unlike prior work that learns monolithic latent embeddings or context-inference without strict compression hierarchies, our approach systematically optimizes for minimal memory usage and scalable zero-shot control on resource-constrained devices.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a causal representation learning framework that explicitly disentangles invariant causal mechanisms from spurious correlations across diverse environmental modalities to enable zero-shot generalization of autonomous driving policies under unseen geographic and weather domain shifts without any fine-tuning.

BACKGROUND:  
Current zero-shot generalization in autonomous driving fails to reliably separate underlying causal factors of driving dynamics from confounding, region-specific correlations, limiting policy transfer to unseen geographic areas with novel road layouts and weather conditions. Existing domain adaptation approaches typically require retraining or rely on domain-specific data, preventing true zero-shot deployment. The open problem is to learn driving policies that generalize zero-shot by isolating invariant causal features that govern vehicle-environment interactions regardless of geographic domain.

APPROACH:  
We propose Invariant Causal Feature Disentanglement for Driving (ICFD), which integrates multi-modal sensor inputs (camera, LiDAR, vehicle dynamics) from multiple geographic domains into a structured latent causal model. Using a combination of invariant risk minimization, causal graph constraints, and multi-environment interventions simulated via data augmentation, ICFD explicitly separates causal driving factors from spurious environment-specific features. The disentangled causal representations then condition a policy network trained with off-policy RL to produce control actions that generalize zero-shot to new geographic regions and weather conditions without retraining.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on a benchmark suite combining CARLA autonomous driving simulator datasets from multiple cities with varying weather and road structures, measuring zero-shot success rate on unseen city-weather combinations. Baselines include recent state-of-the-art zero-shot RL generalization methods (Inferring Behavior-Specific Context, PERM/PPPO, and the original invariant causal feature extraction method). Metrics include driving success rate, collision rates, and policy robustness under domain shifts.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either learns contextual cues or applies domain adaptation with fine-tuning, ICFD explicitly learns a disentangled causal latent space invariant across geographic and environmental domains, enabling true zero-shot transfer without reliance on domain-specific data or post-training adaptation. This extends the winning direction by rigorously leveraging causal disentanglement combined with multi-modal sensor fusion and intervention-based regularization for robust geographic generalization in RL policies.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Develop a causally grounded latent variable model that explicitly disentangles and imputes missing or corrupted observations in partially observable environments to enable provably robust zero-shot policy adaptation across heterogeneous patient subpopulations in healthcare.

BACKGROUND: Existing zero-shot generalization methods in reinforcement learning inadequately handle partial observability and missing data, especially in complex real-world domains like healthcare where electronic health records (EHRs) are often incomplete and noisy. Current latent causal representation approaches improve robustness but lack formal mechanisms to impute missingness or guarantee policy performance when deployed zero-shot on previously unseen patient subpopulations with distinct missingness patterns or corrupted modalities.

APPROACH: We propose a Partial Observability Causal Imputation Network (POCIN) that jointly learns a structural causal model over latent patient states and an imputation model for missing clinical measurements, grounded in counterfactual inference principles. POCIN integrates (1) a causal encoder-decoder that factors latent states into independent causal mechanisms, (2) a missingness-aware inference network that imputes corrupted or missing observations conditioned on inferred causal states, and (3) a downstream RL policy trained on the disentangled latent space with augmented imputation uncertainty estimates to robustly adapt treatment strategies zero-shot to new subpopulations without fine-tuning.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on multiple synthetic and real-world partially observable healthcare datasets (MIMIC-III with artificially induced missingness, and a simulated ICU environment with variable observation corruption). Baselines include Partial Observability-Aware Causal Representation (the tournament winner), context-aware RL, and state-of-the-art zero-shot offline RL methods. Metrics: zero-shot policy return, robustness to missingness severity, and fidelity of imputed clinical variables.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that models partial observability passively or ignores structured imputation, POCIN explicitly integrates causal imputation with latent state inference to provide principled uncertainty-aware policy adaptation zero-shot. This dual causal-imputation approach enables stronger robustness guarantees and better generalization across heterogeneous missingness patterns unseen during training.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Develop a scalable batch-trained multi-agent meta-world model framework that learns disentangled, causal agent-environment factors enabling zero-shot generalization to completely unseen large-scale urban layouts and dynamic traffic patterns without any fine-tuning.

BACKGROUND: Current zero-shot generalization (ZSG) methods in multi-agent reinforcement learning struggle to scale beyond small environments or single-agent settings, limiting applicability to realistic smart city traffic management. The open problem is how to learn generalized policies that transfer directly and robustly to novel, large urban layouts with complex multi-agent interactions and heterogeneous traffic dynamics without retraining.

APPROACH: We propose a batch training pipeline that jointly trains multi-agent policies alongside a meta-world model capturing causal latent factors of agent behaviors and environment dynamics across diverse urban traffic scenarios. The model explicitly disentangles agent-intrinsic factors, traffic rule constraints, and map topology via structured latent representations learned through variational inference and causal discovery objectives. The learned latent space conditions agent policies via a shared context encoder, enabling zero-shot transfer to new city layouts and traffic patterns by sampling unseen latent combinations. Multi-agent coordination is facilitated by a centralized training and decentralized execution scheme using graph neural networks to handle variable agent counts and road network topologies.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluation will be conducted on a suite of large-scale smart city traffic simulators with a held-out set of novel map layouts and heterogeneous traffic demands. Baselines include single-agent meta-world models, modular multi-agent policies without causal disentanglement, and recent ZSG MARL methods such as MIGSS and equilibrium policy generalization frameworks. Metrics include average traffic throughput, congestion levels, multi-agent coordination efficiency (e.g., intersection collision rates, wait times), and zero-shot policy robustness measured by performance degradation on unseen layouts.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior methods limited to small-scale or single-agent settings, this approach explicitly learns disentangled causal latent factors across multi-agent and environment interactions at city-scale, enabling scalable zero-shot generalization without retraining. It combines meta-world modeling with causal representation learning and graph-based coordination, a synergy absent from existing work that either focuses on context embedding, equilibrium heuristics, or modular subtask decomposition but lacks scalable batch-trained, causal multi-agent frameworks for zero-shot transfer in large urban traffic domains.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Develop a domain- and safety-constrained energy-aware pruning framework that jointly optimizes reinforcement learning policy architectures for zero-shot generalization and guaranteed reliable control on wearable health monitors under unseen clinical conditions.

BACKGROUND: Existing energy-efficient pruning methods for RL policies on wearable devices focus on minimizing energy consumption but overlook strict clinical safety constraints critical for reliable zero-shot generalization in unseen health scenarios. The challenge remains to systematically incorporate domain-specific reliability guarantees during model compression while maintaining low-latency inference and extended battery life in real-world clinical deployments.

APPROACH: We propose Safety-Constrained Structured Pruning (SCSP), which integrates formal safety verification and domain-specific constraint modules into the pruning pipeline, ensuring pruned policies satisfy worst-case clinical performance thresholds before deployment. SCSP jointly optimizes pruning masks, low-power architecture parameters, and policy robustness objectives via a multi-objective optimization scheme incorporating a differentiable safety loss derived from domain constraints and zero-shot generalization metrics estimated from out-of-distribution clinical simulators.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate SCSP on benchmark wearable health monitoring RL tasks including glucose regulation and arrhythmia detection, using realistic zero-shot clinical scenarios simulated via physiological models. Compare against state-of-the-art pruning, policy compression, and domain-agnostic zero-shot RL baselines using metrics of battery life extension, inference latency, zero-shot policy reliability (worst-case performance under distribution shift), and clinical safety violation rates.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior pruning methods that do not enforce domain-specific safety constraints or explicitly optimize for zero-shot generalization reliability, SCSP introduces a formal safety-aware pruning framework tailored for wearable health RL control, simultaneously achieving provable reliability guarantees, energy efficiency, and robust zero-shot clinical performance.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA: Develop a hybrid quantum-classical memory-aware quantum kernel framework that dynamically adapts quantum circuit depth and classical cache usage during training to optimize classification accuracy under strict, jointly constrained quantum and classical memory budgets for edge AI.

BACKGROUND: Existing quantum kernel methods for edge AI target minimizing either quantum resource consumption or classical memory footprint but do not jointly optimize or dynamically adapt these resources during training and inference. There is a lack of systematic methods to trade off quantum circuit complexity (qubit count and depth) with classical cache usage to maximize accuracy under real-world edge memory constraints, limiting practical deployment on severely resource-limited devices.

APPROACH: Introduce a memory-adaptive quantum kernel training algorithm that iteratively adjusts quantum circuit depth and classical kernel matrix caching based on measured memory usage feedback, using a multi-objective constrained optimization approach to respect combined quantum and classical memory budgets. The quantum feature maps employ parameterized shallow circuits with controllable depth and qubit reuse to minimize quantum register size, while classical kernel caching employs selective compression and sparse approximation techniques to reduce footprint without degrading kernel quality. Resource usage is profiled at each training step in a simulated edge environment to guide adaptation.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on standard image classification datasets including MNIST and Fashion-MNIST under simulated edge hardware constraints reflecting typical IoT device memory limits. Compare classification accuracy, total memory consumption (quantum + classical), and training/inference latency against state-of-the-art fixed-memory quantum kernel methods and classical kernel baselines.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that statically designs quantum kernels with fixed resource budgets or focuses solely on either quantum or classical memory reduction, this method dynamically balances and adapts quantum circuit and classical cache resources during kernel training to maximize accuracy under joint memory constraints, enabling practical deployment on memory-constrained edge devices.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA: Develop an adaptive hybrid quantum-classical training framework that dynamically reallocates computational resources between quantum circuit evaluation and classical optimization under strict latency constraints to achieve provable quantum advantage in real-time control tasks.

BACKGROUND: Existing hybrid quantum-classical training pipelines optimize either quantum or classical latency components individually, lacking a unified adaptive strategy to balance these under real-time latency budgets. The open problem is how to dynamically and jointly optimize quantum circuit depth, gate selection, and classical optimizer steps in a latency-aware manner that enables practical quantum advantage in low-latency autonomous control scenarios.

APPROACH: We propose an end-to-end adaptive scheduler that adjusts quantum circuit parametrization (circuit depth, non-Clifford gate count) and classical optimizer iteration budget in real time based on measured latency and control performance feedback. This scheduler leverages a latency-constrained multi-objective optimization formulation to allocate computational effort dynamically to quantum or classical components, ensuring adherence to strict latency budgets while maximizing control accuracy. The method integrates quantum resource-aware pruning techniques and lightweight surrogate models for rapid latency and accuracy prediction during training.

EXPERIMENT: Validate the approach on standard autonomous control benchmarks (e.g., cart-pole balancing, drone navigation simulators) using quantum hardware emulators and specialized latency measurement frameworks. Evaluate end-to-end latency, control task success rate, and training convergence speed against fixed-budget hybrid pipelines and classical-only baselines.
